paragraphs.text: (PG)
POLISH GAMES ASSOCIATION
Warsaw, 23 October, 2025

On behalf of Stowarzyszenie Polskie Gry (Polish Games Association), further referred to as "SPG", we welcome the opportunity to contribute to the European Commission's public consultation on the forthcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA). We understand the importance of consumer protection in the digital environment, and we would like to offer additional perspective on the matter.

section_path: Submission to the European Commission's Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: SPG was established in 2015 and brings together both medium-sized and the largest video game developers in Poland. SPG members include CD PROJEKT S.A. (developer of the "The Witcher" and "Cyberpunk 2077" series), Techland S.A. (studio known for titles such as the "Dying Light" and "Call of Juarez" series), 11 bit studios S.A. (the company behind games such as "Frostpunk" and "This War of Mine"), Bloober Team S.A. (developer responsible for "Medium" and "Cronos: The New Dawn"), People Can Fly S.A. (creators of games such as "Bulletstorm" and "Outriders"), and Flying Wild Hog sp. z o.o. (studio responsible for "Space Punks" and the "Shadow Warrior" series). SPG works closely with representatives of the Polish government, entrepreneurs, non-profit organizations, and, as a member of the European Game Developers Federation (EGDF), also with other creative industry associations.

section_path: SPG and the Polish video games sector

evidence_quotes.quote: the Polish video game sector

paragraphs.text: Our industry is one of the largest branches of the Polish creative sector. According to the report published by the Polish Agency for Enterprise Development (PARP) $^1$ , in 2023 there were nearly 500 game developers operating in Poland, and the Polish industry released over 500 titles during this period, $97\%$ of which were exported. In terms of employment, the Polish video game sector is the second largest market in the EU after France, ahead of Germany, Spain, and Sweden.

section_path: SPG and the Polish video games sector

evidence_quotes.quote: Polish video games also enjoy artistic success, winning prestigious awards and distinctions.

paragraphs.text: Apart from the significant commercial achievements and cultural significance, Polish video games also enjoy artistic success, winning prestigious awards and distinctions. Our sector also contributes to supporting human capital through educational activities - game developers co-create numerous conferences, events, and programs organized in Poland, such as the Promised Land Art Festival, Digital Dragons, and the Game Industry Conference.

section_path: SPG and the Polish video games sector

paragraphs.text: The Game Industry of Poland – Report 2023 - https://en.parp.gov.pl/storage/publications/pdf/The_Game_Industry_Poland_2023_12_07.pdf

section_path: SPG and the Polish video games sector

paragraphs.text: < PG> POLISH GAMES ASSOCIATION

section_path: SPG and the Polish video games sector

paragraphs.text: In light of the European Commission's ongoing consultation on DFA, we respectfully urge caution in its development and implementation. While we fully support the overarching goal of ensuring fairness and transparency in digital markets for all consumers, including players, we believe that the current regulatory landscape already provides a robust framework for consumer protection in the digital domain.

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

paragraphs.text: The European Union has enacted several key directives that address consumer rights and commercial fairness, including:

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

paragraphs.text: Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (2005/29/EC)

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

paragraphs.text: Consumer Rights Directive (2011/83/EU)

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

evidence_quotes.quote: Unfair Contract Terms Directive (93/13/EEC)

paragraphs.text: Unfair Contract Terms Directive (93/13/EEC)

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

evidence_quotes.quote: deceptive practices
unfair terms

paragraphs.text: These foundational instruments have been further reinforced by recent legislation such as the Digital Content Directive, Digital Services Act and the Digital Markets Act. Together, these laws already cover a wide spectrum of consumer protection issues, including deceptive practices, unfair terms, and digital platform accountability.

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

evidence_quotes.quote: video game development

paragraphs.text: Introducing another major legislative act in the same area risks regulatory compliance fatigue, particularly for industries such as video game development, which are already navigating complex compliance obligations. The gaming sector is a vibrant and culturally significant part of Europe's creative economy. Studios – especially small and medium-sized ones – must focus their resources on creative content development, which is their core business. Additional regulatory burdens will inevitably divert attention and resources away from innovation and artistic expression.

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns, addictive design
influencer marketing

paragraphs.text: Moreover, the costs of compliance with new, potentially overlapping rules could disproportionately affect EU-based studios, making them less competitive compared to their counterparts in jurisdictions with more streamlined regulatory environments. This is especially concerning given that many of the issues targeted by DFA – such as dark patterns, addictive design, and influencer marketing – are already addressed under existing EU law and enforcement mechanisms. Additionally, it goes against the Commission's own priority to increase Europe's competitiveness on the global market through, in particular, simplifying legislation and eliminating overlaps.

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

evidence_quotes.quote: PEGI (Pan European Game Information) system, which has been widely adopted by leading studios and provides effective age rating and content guidance.

paragraphs.text: We also advocate for greater recognition of self-regulatory frameworks, such as the PEGI (Pan European Game Information) system, which has been widely adopted by leading studios and provides effective age rating and content guidance. PEGI demonstrates that the industry is capable of responsible

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

paragraphs.text: 《PG》 POLISH GAMES ASSOCIATION

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

paragraphs.text: conduct without the need for excessive legislative intervention. Self-regulatory frameworks allow for selecting more proportional measures, adjusted to the specifics of the industry and leading to the intended goal without impeding creativity.

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

evidence_quotes.quote: Video games are not merely commercial products – they are interactive narratives, educational tools, and social experiences.
Overly prescriptive rules could undermine the creative freedom that is essential to the medium's continued growth and relevance.

paragraphs.text: Finally, we caution against overregulation that may inadvertently stifle a unique form of artistic and cultural expression. Video games are not merely commercial products – they are interactive narratives, educational tools, and social experiences. Overly prescriptive rules could undermine the creative freedom that is essential to the medium's continued growth and relevance.

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

paragraphs.text: We therefore urge the Commission to:

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

paragraphs.text: Conduct a thorough impact assessment with specific attention to the gaming sector

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

evidence_quotes.quote: Avoid duplicative or overly burdensome provisions

paragraphs.text: Avoid duplicative or overly burdensome provisions

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

paragraphs.text: Promote harmonization and simplification rather than expansion of regulatory scope

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

paragraphs.text: Encourage co-regulation and self-regulation where appropriate

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

paragraphs.text: We remain committed to constructive dialogue and welcome the opportunity to contribute to a balanced and effective consumer protection framework that supports both fairness and innovation.

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

paragraphs.text: Following these general remarks, we would like to elaborate on selected regulation objectives declared by the Commission or policy issues raised in response to the call for evidence.

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

evidence_quotes.quote: Dark patterns
unfair practices related to the price

paragraphs.text: Dark patterns and and other unfair techniques that pressure, deceive and manipulate consumers online as well as addressing unfair practices related to the price

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns and other deceptive practices

paragraphs.text: We believe dark patterns and other deceptive practices are already sufficiently regulated within the EU regulatory landscape. The existence of dark patterns in video games is purely a matter of the law being unenforced. Moreover, introducing more regulation on the same subject matter without improvement in the enforcement of consumer law regulation may actually cause confusion for all actors involved, introducing (among other) a false sense of safety in the consumer.

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

evidence_quotes.quote: the term "dark pattern" should be understood broadly to encompass any user experience (UX) design patterns that are used as tricks to exploit and manipulate users.
any practice that is designed to manipulate or deceive the player for commercial gain to be a dark pattern

paragraphs.text: Following the EU and worldwide jurisprudence, we believe the term "dark pattern" should be understood broadly to encompass any user experience (UX) design patterns that are used as tricks to exploit and manipulate users. In other words, SPG would consider any practice that is designed to manipulate or deceive the player for commercial gain to be a dark pattern and, as such, to be already regulated by the UCPD Article 5 as a practice that distorts the economic behaviour of a consumer and goes against

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

paragraphs.text: 《PG》 POLISH GAMES ASSOCIATION

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: professional diligence of a reputable game provider. Naming specific dark patterns in an ever expanding, overly specific catalogue is not going to solve the problem – it will only create confusion with regards to which practice is banned and which is not. We believe malicious actors on the market that try to exploit players through deception will be faster in inventing new types of dark patterns than the EU regulatory bodies in prohibiting their use.

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns in games

paragraphs.text: SPG does acknowledge an existing problem of dark patterns in games, but believes the issue is not in the lack of regulation, but in ineffective enforcement. Given the appropriate tools and know-how, local authorities can fight deceptive behaviour towards players based on existing regulation, as can be evidenced by the activity of the The Netherlands Authority for Consumers and Markets, as well as CPC Network.

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

evidence_quotes.quote: The problem of dark patterns in games is therefore purely an enforcement matter.
loot boxes containing randomised rewards are considered gambling in Belgium (and therefore banned without a proper licence) since 2018
enforcement rules for the enforcement of UCPD Article 5 in games.

paragraphs.text: The problem of dark patterns in games is therefore purely an enforcement matter. Conversely, loot boxes containing randomised rewards are considered gambling in Belgium (and therefore banned without a proper licence) since 2018 and yet the study made in 2022 reveals 82% of game providers continued to generate revenue through a randomised monetisation method. We believe the similar pattern may repeat in the case of further regulation of dark patterns under DFA. Instead of another regulation on the subject matter, the EU should focus on clear and effective enforcement rules for the enforcement of UCPD Article 5 in games.

section_path: General remarks – A call for caution

evidence_quotes.quote: exploitative designs aimed at manipulating players to their economic detriment.
The video game industry cautions that high engagement is inherent to the medium's appeal.

paragraphs.text: The video game industry cautions that high engagement is inherent to the medium's appeal. The very design elements that make a game fun – challenging goals, rewards, social interaction, an absorbing story – are the same elements that keep players wanting to continue playing. In this sense, a game has to be compelling to be enjoyable. Demanding that games be made "less addictive" is akin to asking snack and sweets makers to make treats less tasty or sport car makers to make vehicles slower and less comfortable – it strikes at the core of product enjoyment. Engaging design and repeat play are normal and desired characteristics of games, not nefarious tricks in themselves. Therefore SPG urges the Commission not to underestimate the importance of discerning between gameplay features that improve the enjoyment and support the compelling interactive experience of the players from exploitative designs aimed at manipulating players to their economic detriment.

section_path: Giving players (and parents) greater control of the online experience by addressing addictive design features that lead consumers, particularly minors, to spend excessive time and money on online goods and services

paragraphs.text: < PG>

section_path: Giving players (and parents) greater control of the online experience by addressing addictive design features that lead consumers, particularly minors, to spend excessive time and money on online goods and services

paragraphs.text: POLISH GAMES ASSOCIATION

section_path: Giving players (and parents) greater control of the online experience by addressing addictive design features that lead consumers, particularly minors, to spend excessive time and money on online goods and services

evidence_quotes.quote: Every major game platform includes robust parental control features that allow families to set boundaries on game time and spending.

paragraphs.text: That said, SPG recognises the importance of empowering players (and parents) to manage their gameplay habits and avoid problematic overuse. We welcome opportunities to discuss and implement measures that give consumers greater control over their gameplay experience. In fact, many such tools are already in place. Every major game platform includes robust parental control features that allow families to set boundaries on game time and spending. For example, console and mobile platforms let parents (or users themselves) limit the daily play duration, require approval or spending limits on in-game purchases, restrict access to age-inappropriate games, and manage online interactions.

section_path: Giving players (and parents) greater control of the online experience by addressing addictive design features that lead consumers, particularly minors, to spend excessive time and money on online goods and services

evidence_quotes.quote: manipulation of personal data to prey on a player's specific weakness

paragraphs.text: SPG agrees that any manipulation of personal data to prey on a player's specific weakness (for example, tailoring offers to an individual known to have a gambling addiction) would be highly unethical. Fortunately, the EU already has a strict framework governing personal data use and profiling in the form of GDPR.

section_path: Giving players (and parents) greater control of the online experience by addressing addictive design features that lead consumers, particularly minors, to spend excessive time and money on online goods and services
Addressing problematic personalisation practices, including situations where consumer vulnerabilities are targeted for the purposes of personalised advertising and pricing

evidence_quotes.quote: what data is collected and how it's used, particularly if any automated decision-making or profiling is involved.
GDPR personalised targeting often requires the user's explicit consent.
automated profiling can pose risks to individuals' rights, so it gives users substantial control.

using any sensitive personal traits (so-called "special categories of data", like health status, ethnicity, etc.) in personalisation is generally forbidden – a company cannot leverage data about, say, someone's mental health or disability to personalise an offer, unless the user has given explicit consent for that specific use.
fully automated personalised decisions with significant effects on a consumer (which could include individualised pricing in certain cases) are constrained by GDPR's Article 22.

paragraphs.text: Firstly, game providers must inform users in a clear, accessible way about what data is collected and how it's used, particularly if any automated decision-making or profiling is involved. Secondly, under GDPR personalised targeting often requires the user's explicit consent. The law recognises that automated profiling can pose risks to individuals' rights, so it gives users substantial control. For instance, using any sensitive personal traits (so-called "special categories of data", like health status, ethnicity, etc.) in personalisation is generally forbidden – a company cannot leverage data about, say, someone's mental health or disability to personalise an offer, unless the user has given explicit consent for that specific use. Even apart from sensitive data, fully automated personalised decisions with significant effects on a consumer (which could include individualised pricing in certain cases) are constrained by GDPR's Article 22.

section_path: Giving players (and parents) greater control of the online experience by addressing addictive design features that lead consumers, particularly minors, to spend excessive time and money on online goods and services
Addressing problematic personalisation practices, including situations where consumer vulnerabilities are targeted for the purposes of personalised advertising and pricing

evidence_quotes.quote: The recent regulatory actions in Europe underscore that consent is the cornerstone for personalised ads.

personalisation based on exploitation of player vulnerabilities could only happen after express consent as a legal basis under GDPR Article 6(1)a, as in this case no other legal basis may be applicable.

paragraphs.text: The recent regulatory actions in Europe underscore that consent is the cornerstone for personalised ads. Notably, after the Urgent Binding Decision 01/2023 requested by the Norwegian SA for the ordering of final measures regarding Meta Platforms Ireland Ltd (Art. 66(2) GDPR), it is clear that personalisation based on exploitation of player vulnerabilities could only happen after express consent as a legal basis under GDPR Article 6(1)a, as in this case no other legal basis may be applicable.

section_path: Giving players (and parents) greater control of the online experience by addressing addictive design features that lead consumers, particularly minors, to spend excessive time and money on online goods and services
Addressing problematic personalisation practices, including situations where consumer vulnerabilities are targeted for the purposes of personalised advertising and pricing

evidence_quotes.quote: influencer marketing industry

paragraphs.text: Members of SPG, who from time to time cooperate with influencers in promotional activities, observe the increasing maturity of the influencer marketing industry, which in recent years has become highly

Influencer marketing

paragraphs.text: professionalised. In this context, SPG considers the current regulatory framework to be adequate and effective, appropriately distributing responsibilities among market participants. We believe that consistent enforcement using the legal instruments already available would be sufficient to achieve the desired regulatory outcomes, without the need to introduce additional, more specific EU-level regulations that could lead to overregulation and reduce necessary flexibility.

section_path: Giving players (and parents) greater control of the online experience by addressing addictive design features that lead consumers, particularly minors, to spend excessive time and money on online goods and services
Influencer marketing

evidence_quotes.quote: influencer marketing

paragraphs.text: In this regard, we would like to highlight the activity of Polish Office of Competition and Consumer Protection (Urząd Ochrony Konkurencji i Konsumenta, UOKiK) related to influencer marketing as a prime example of a comprehensive approach addressing the relevant issues while operating within the existing EU and domestic legal frameworks.

section_path: Giving players (and parents) greater control of the online experience by addressing addictive design features that lead consumers, particularly minors, to spend excessive time and money on online goods and services
Influencer marketing

evidence_quotes.quote: labeling advertising content by influencers

paragraphs.text: In 2022, in cooperation with relevant industry associations and representatives of the academia, UOKiK prepared a comprehensive, practical and accessible guidance on the labeling advertising content by influencers. This initiative was subsequently followed by extensive enforcement actions in the following years. Owing to these concentrated efforts and the regulator's focus on a wide group of high-profile influencers as subjects of enforcement proceedings, UOKiK succeeded not only in mobilising market participants directly affected by the regulation but also in raising public awareness of the guidelines, thereby creating additional pressure to ensure compliance.

section_path: Giving players (and parents) greater control of the online experience by addressing addictive design features that lead consumers, particularly minors, to spend excessive time and money on online goods and services
Influencer marketing

evidence_quotes.quote: influencer marketing

paragraphs.text: Considering the above, SPG encourages the Commission to refrain from introducing additional provisions regarding influencer marketing in the DFA, where consistent enforcement of existing rules and the promotion of self-regulatory initiatives would adequately address the identified challenges.

section_path: Giving players (and parents) greater control of the online experience by addressing addictive design features that lead consumers, particularly minors, to spend excessive time and money on online goods and services
Influencer marketing

evidence_quotes.quote: video games

paragraphs.text: SPG recognises the European Citizens' Initiative called Stop Destroying Videogames and appreciates the discussion it has sparked in relation to problematic features in digital products such as video games, which is one of the areas studied under this call for evidence. It is impossible not to notice numerous statements brought by gamers in this public consultation, after the initiative has collected over 1.4 million statements of support, which is why we expect the Initiative to be discussed in the perspective of the Digital Fairness Act legislative process.

section_path: European Citizens' Initiative "Stop Destroying Videogames"

paragraphs.text: SPG understands gamers' concerns and shares their love of video games – SPG is formed of people who joined the video games industry out of their passion for games and therefore it believes in a unique potential of video games as a form of art, entertainment and timeless narration.

section_path: European Citizens' Initiative "Stop Destroying Videogames"

paragraphs.text: < PG> POLISH GAMES ASSOCIATION

section_path: European Citizens' Initiative "Stop Destroying Videogames"

evidence_quotes.quote: video games preservation matters to European citizens

paragraphs.text: SPG supports all actions that aid the development of the video games industry, development of new titles and proliferation of games in culture, education, including through games preservation – archival actions enabling games to be available for future gamers, researchers and collectors. Therefore, SPG believes that the proposals forming the basis of this action deserve to be discussed. The initiative is a clear signal for the industry that should spark reflection and discussion about concrete actions. It is also a clear proof video games preservation matters to European citizens and it deserves to be supported not only by the industry itself, but also – if not primarily – by public European and national bodies, to make sure that video games, as a part of pan-European and national cultures, are supported at least on the same level as other forms of culture such as films or music.

section_path: European Citizens' Initiative "Stop Destroying Videogames"

evidence_quotes.quote: licensing restrictions.

paragraphs.text: At the same time, SPG believes that the discussion should include the voice of actual people creating video games and space for touching on restrictions or challenges they might be facing – such as cost of maintaining services, risks associated with opening up server infrastructure, or licensing restrictions. SPG also recognises that the video games industry is incredibly dynamic and creative and therefore challenges faced can vary greatly from game to game, which makes it very challenging to provide a one-size-fits-all solution to the issues raised.

section_path: European Citizens' Initiative "Stop Destroying Videogames"

paragraphs.text: SPG believes that in the discussion between all the interested parties, it is possible to work out solutions that address the issues raised by gamers, through the Citizens' Initiative or otherwise. SPG welcomes the dialogue on the topic and believes in opportunity for self-regulation to provide solutions that are possible to implement and fit for each game instead of introducing additional legal requirements which go against the purpose of levelling the playing field for European businesses in the video games industry, one of a few stellar examples of European success stories at the intersection of culture, tech, and startups.

section_path: European Citizens' Initiative "Stop Destroying Videogames"

paragraphs.text: In conclusion, SPG believes the necessary regulation is already in place and introduction of new regulation is only going to confuse the game providers and players alike. Instead of introducing new regulation, SPG suggests using this opportunity to discuss how to enforce the existing laws, starting dialogue between the industry and enforcement bodies and creating regulatory safe harbours for the business to follow.

section_path: European Citizens' Initiative "Stop Destroying Videogames"
Final remarks
